{
  "gaps": [
    {
      "id": "GAP-BOR-001",
      "title": "Bill of Rights completely undefined – no substantive enterprise scope or constraints",
      "description": "All six articles of the Bill of Rights (BoR) contain only the placeholder text 'To be defined by human principal'. The BoR is the foundational human-authored document that defines the DIO's scope, prohibited activities, core identity, and interpretation rules. Without its content, the Arbiter's scope gate (BoR perimeter check) cannot enforce any boundaries, making the entire compliance architecture effectively non-functional.",
      "grounding": "Bill of Rights Articles I, II, III, IV, V, VI (all marked 'To be defined') and MSP section 'Relationship to the Bill of Rights' which states the MSP is strictly subordinate to the BoR and any conflicting MSP rule is void – an empty BoR undermines this precedence.",
      "criticality": "critical",
      "urgency": "high",
      "impact": "very high",
      "rationale": "Critical because the BoR is the sole source of the DIO's lawful scope; without it, no action proposal can be meaningfully perimeter-checked. Urgency is high as the system is in an active bootstrap state with a permissive MSP, but the risk is latent until actions are dispatched. Impact is very high because the absence of defined scope could allow unauthorized or harmful operations once the DIO begins executing goals."
    },
    {
      "id": "GAP-BOR-002",
      "title": "No BoR hash registration event recorded in governance state",
      "description": "The Governance State Slice is empty, indicating that no `bor_version` concept has been written to the Graph. The BoR document mandates that its SHA-256 hash be registered as a `bor_version` concept before Arbiter will accept it. Without a registered hash, Arbiter (#190) cannot verify the BoR at scope queries, potentially either blocking all actions or defaulting to an insecure verification bypass.",
      "grounding": "BoR 'Hash Registration' section and Arbiter Reference in the MSP ('Arbiter (#190) verifies the Bill of Rights at every scope query; it does NOT verify the MSP'). Also MSP 'Relationship to the Bill of Rights' paragraphs regarding Arbiter's verification mechanism.",
      "criticality": "high",
      "urgency": "high",
      "impact": "high",
      "rationale": "High criticality because the Arbiter's scope gate is a mandatory first compliance gate; if it cannot verify the BoR, the entire Nomos adjudication pipeline is broken. Urgency is high because the system is already in operation (seed version) and any scope query would encounter this state. Impact is high – the Arbiter would either fail-safe (blocking all legitimate actions) or fail-open (allowing unverified actions), both of which are severe compliance deviations."
    }
  ]
}

{
  "gaps": [
    {
      "article": "Bill of Rights Article I — Scope of Enterprise",
      "section": null,
      "description": "The article is completely undefined ('[To be defined by human principal]'). Without a scope of enterprise, there is no constitutional boundary for agent actions, and the Arbiter (#190) cannot determine whether any action proposal falls within or exceeds the organism's permitted remit. The resulting vacuum renders all enterprise activities either arbitrarily blocked or arbitrarily permitted, depending on implementation, which is a critical integrity failure for an agentic DIO.",
      "criticality": "critical",
      "urgency": "critical",
      "impact": "Any action proposal submitted to Nomos would lack a valid scope gate, making the entire compliance pipeline non-deterministic or unsafe. This undermines the very purpose of the BoR as a safety framework.",
      "priority": 1
    },
    {
      "article": "Bill of Rights Article II — Prohibited Activities",
      "section": null,
      "description": "The article is undefined. There is no codified list of forbidden actions, creating a constitutional vacuum where no activity is explicitly off-limits. This defeats the BoR's role in encoding inviolable 'never' constraints, leaving the organism without ethical or safety redlines.",
      "criticality": "critical",
      "urgency": "critical",
      "impact": "The organism can drift into any action, including dangerous ones, without a constitutional barrier. Combined with the undefined scope, it renders the BoR irrelevant to safety enforcement.",
      "priority": 2
    },
    {
      "article": "Bill of Rights Article III — Core Identity",
      "section": null,
      "description": "The article is undefined. The organism has no stated identity, purpose, or immutable characteristics. This is not just a missing philosophical anchor — it means alignment, role, and self-modeling are constitutionally vacuous, making it impossible to detect drift or mission-inconsistent behavior at the constitutional level.",
      "criticality": "critical",
      "urgency": "high",
      "impact": "Without core identity, the organism's long-term development cannot be anchored to stable designer intent, increasing the risk of unbounded self-modification and goal misgeneralization.",
      "priority": 3
    },
    {
      "article": "Bill of Rights Article IV — Human Authority Reservation",
      "section": null,
      "description": "The article is undefined. The BoR does not specify which powers are reserved exclusively for the human principal. This leaves open the possibility of automated systems encroaching on decisions that must remain under human control, circumventing the intended human governance model.",
      "criticality": "high",
      "urgency": "high",
      "impact": "Loss of a clear human-in-the-loop guarantee. Automated organs might drift into legislating or interpreting the BoR itself without explicit human authorization, accelerating goal erosion.",
      "priority": 4
    },
    {
      "article": "Bill of Rights Article V — Amendment Protocol",
      "section": null,
      "description": "The article is undefined. There is no constitutional process for amending the BoR itself. The MSP notes that BoR amendments are human-only, but without a defined protocol (including quorum, review, activation), the BoR is frozen in an unusable scaffold state with no legitimate path to completion.",
      "criticality": "high",
      "urgency": "critical",
      "impact": "The organism cannot lawfully transition from bootstrap to a governed state. Every other gap remains permanently open because the mechanism to close them is missing, blocking all constitutional maturation.",
      "priority": 5
    },
    {
      "article": "Bill of Rights Article VI — Interpretation Rules",
      "section": null,
      "description": "The article is undefined. There are no rules for resolving conflicts between articles, handling edge cases, or determining the spirit of the law. In combination with an undefined scope, this makes constitutional interpretation impossible for both the Arbiter and Nomos.",
      "criticality": "medium",
      "urgency": "medium",
      "impact": "Even after the other articles are defined, interpretative ambiguity could lead to deadlocks or inconsistent enforcement, weakening the entire constitutional framework.",
      "priority": 6
    }
  ]
}
